lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: On Thursday, sir? the time is very short.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: My father Capulet will have it so;

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: And I am nothing slow to slack his haste.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: You say you do not know the lady’s mind:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Uneven is the course, I like it not.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Immoderately she weeps for Tybalt’s death,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: And therefore have I little talk’d of love;

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: For Venus smiles not in a house of tears.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Now, sir, her father counts it dangerous

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: That she doth give her sorrow so much sway,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: And in his wisdom hastes our marriage,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: To stop the inundation of her tears;

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Which, too much minded by herself alone,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: May be put from her by society:

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Now do you know the reason of this haste.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: I would I knew not why it should be slow’d.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Look, sir, here comes the lady towards my cell.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Happily met, my lady and my wife!

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That may be, sir, when I may be a wife.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: That may be must be, love, on Thursday next.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What must be shall be.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: That’s a certain text.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Come you to make confession to this father?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To answer that, I should confess to you.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Do not deny to him that you love me.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I will confess to you that I love him.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: So will ye, I am sure, that you love me.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: If I do so, it will be of more price,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Being spoke behind your back, than to your face.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Poor soul, thy face is much abused with tears.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: The tears have got small victory by that;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: For it was bad enough before their spite.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Thou wrong’st it, more than tears, with that report.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: That is no slander, sir, which is a truth;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And what I spake, I spake it to my face.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Thy face is mine, and thou hast slander’d it.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: It may be so, for it is not mine own.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Are you at leisure, holy father, now;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or shall I come to you at evening mass?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: My leisure serves me, pensive daughter, now.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: My lord, we must entreat the time alone.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: God shield I should disturb devotion!

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Juliet, on Thursday early will I rouse ye:

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Till then, adieu; and keep this holy kiss.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O shut the door! and when thou hast done so,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Come weep with me; past hope, past cure, past help!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Ah, Juliet, I already know thy grief;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: It strains me past the compass of my wits:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: I hear thou must, and nothing may prorogue it,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: On Thursday next be married to this county.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Tell me not, friar, that thou hear’st of this,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Unless thou tell me how I may prevent it:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: If, in thy wisdom, thou canst give no help,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Do thou but call my resolution wise,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And with this knife I’ll help it presently.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: God join’d my heart and Romeo’s, thou our hands;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And ere this hand, by thee to Romeo seal’d,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Shall be the label to another deed,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or my true heart with treacherous revolt

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Turn to another, this shall slay them both:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Therefore, out of thy long-experienced time,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Give me some present counsel, or, behold,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: ’Twixt my extremes and me this bloody knife

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Shall play the umpire, arbitrating that

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Which the commission of thy years and art

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Could to no issue of true honour bring.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Be not so long to speak; I long to die,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: If what thou speak’st speak not of remedy.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Hold, daughter: I do spy a kind of hope,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Which craves as desperate an execution

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: As that is desperate which we would prevent.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: If, rather than to marry County Paris,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thou hast the strength of will to slay thyself,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Then is it likely thou wilt undertake

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: A thing like death to chide away this shame,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: That copest with death himself to scape from it;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And, if thou darest, I’ll give thee remedy.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O, bid me leap, rather than marry Paris,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: From off the battlements of yonder tower;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or walk in thievish ways; or bid me lurk

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Where serpents are; chain me with roaring bears;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or shut me nightly in a charnel-house,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O’er-cover’d quite with dead men’s rattling bones,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: With reeky shanks and yellow chapless skulls;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Or bid me go into a new-made grave

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And hide me with a dead man in his shroud;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Things that, to hear them told, have made me tremble;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And I will do it without fear or doubt,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To live an unstain’d wife to my sweet love.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Hold, then; go home, be merry, give consent

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To marry Paris: Wednesday is to-morrow:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To-morrow night look that thou lie alone;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Let not thy nurse lie with thee in thy chamber:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Take thou this vial, being then in bed,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And this distilled liquor drink thou off;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: When presently through all thy veins shall run

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: A cold and drowsy humour, for no pulse

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Shall keep his native progress, but surcease:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: No warmth, no breath, shall testify thou livest;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The roses in thy lips and cheeks shall fade

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To paly ashes, thy eyes’ windows fall,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Like death, when he shuts up the day of life;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Each part, deprived of supple government,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Shall, stiff and stark and cold, appear like death:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And in this borrow’d likeness of shrunk death

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thou shalt continue two and forty hours,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And then awake as from a pleasant sleep.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Now, when the bridegroom in the morning comes

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To rouse thee from thy bed, there art thou dead:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Then, as the manner of our country is,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: In thy best robes uncover’d on the bier

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thou shalt be borne to that same ancient vault

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Where all the kindred of the Capulets lie.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: In the meantime, against thou shalt awake,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Shall Romeo by my letters know our drift,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And hither shall he come: and he and I

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Will watch thy waking, and that very night

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Shall Romeo bear thee hence to Mantua.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And this shall free thee from this present shame;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: If no inconstant toy, nor womanish fear,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Abate thy valour in the acting it.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Give me, give me! O, tell not me of fear!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Hold; get you gone, be strong and prosperous

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: In this resolve: I’ll send a friar with speed

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To Mantua, with my letters to thy lord.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Love give me strength! and strength shall help afford.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Farewell, dear father!

glosses.definition: slow him down
thought of; considered

company

glosses.phrase: now do you know the reason of this haste
glosses.definition: Paris asserts the wedding is Capulet's remedy for Juliet's grief

glosses.phrase: I would I knew not why it should be slow’d
glosses.definition: I wish I didn't know reason to delay
well met

glosses.phrase: I will confess to you that I love him
glosses.definition: Juliet tells Paris she loves someone — but the Friar is her audience
directly in my presence

past the reach of my intellect — the Friar's despair

kill both obstacles — Juliet rejects living with Paris

she would brave any horror to avoid marrying Paris
wild bears — any monster rather than Paris
a building where human bones and corpses are stored
stinking leg-bones
bare jaw-less skulls — decayed yellow remains

forty-two hours — the Friar's cardinal time-estimate
the frame on which a coffin is carried
the old family burial chamber — the Capulet tomb
in preparation for when you wake
the city of Romeo's exile — about twenty-five miles from Verona
changeable whim — let no whim stop you

annotations.title: The Friar's plan — applied ethics of medicine and poison
annotations.body: The Friar's plan is the direct application of the philosophy he announced in 2.3: 'Within the infant rind of this weak flower / Poison hath residence and medicine power.' Juliet will drink a draught that mimics death, 'for two and forty hours,' long enough for her burial and for Romeo to arrive to find her alive in the tomb. The plan is intricate, physically specific, and depends on timing. Shakespeare makes the Friar name every detail: the draught's chill, the forty-two hours, Juliet's appearance of death, the waiting at the tomb. Every element will go wrong. Friar John will be quarantined by plague and fail to deliver the letter to Romeo; Romeo will reach the tomb believing Juliet dead and kill himself before she wakes; the Friar will arrive too late. The play does not blame the Friar for wickedness; it indicts him for faith in his own ingenuity in a world that does not cooperate. The lesson: a good plan is not the same as a good outcome.

annotations.title: Juliet's catalogue of horrors — what she will face rather than marry Paris
annotations.body: Before the Friar suggests the potion, Juliet offers him a list of what she would do rather than marry Paris: leap from a tower, walk thievish roads, hide with serpents, be chained with roaring bears, shut in a charnel-house among reeky shanks and yellow chapless skulls, lie in a new-made grave. The catalogue is gothic in the extreme — and it is a rehearsal. Juliet is imagining the tomb she will lie in two scenes later. Shakespeare builds her resolve through speech: by articulating the horror in advance, she makes the actual horror bearable. Notice the poetic weight: the 'reeky shanks and yellow chapless skulls' will be among her literal neighbors when she wakes up.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

trials.prompt: How long is the Friar's draught meant to keep Juliet in death-like sleep?
How does the Friar's plan embody his 2.3 philosophy?
options: It doesn't — the plan contradicts his philosophy
A single agent (the potion) is simultaneously medicine (saving Juliet from Paris) and poison (a near-death) — use and timing determine which
It is a strictly religious rite

trials.prompt: Juliet's catalogue of horrors in 4.1 functions as —
options: A complaint she intends to withdraw
A rehearsal — the images she lists (skulls, reeky shanks, new-made graves) are literally what she will encounter in the tomb
Mere hyperbole with no bearing on later scenes
A plea to postpone the Paris marriage indefinitely
